text: The surgeon performs a total laparoscopic hysterectomy, beginning with the control of the lateral attachment of the uterus. The round ligament is cut, and both ovaries are conserved.

The surgeon performs a salpingectomy, carefully removing the fallopian tube away from the ovary to preserve ovarian vascularization. The utero-ovarian ligament is controlled using bipolar cautery and then cut with a harmonic ace. A window is created to increase the distance between the ureter and the uterus.

The surgeon performs vesicovaginal dissection by pulling the uterus toward the sacrum and cranially retracting the bladder, revealing the vesicovaginal plane. The dissection progresses until the anterior valve of the uterine manipulator is reached, clearly exposing the anterior aspect of the vagina. The same procedure is planned for the right side.

The surgeon controls the round ligament one centimeter outside the uterine cornea using the Harmonic Ace, which cuts the vascular ligament easily. The peritoneal dissection continues, followed by the left salpingectomy. The mesosalpinx is cut two centimeters away from the ovary to preserve ovarian vascularization.

The surgeon uses a bipolar device to control and cut the right utero-ovarian ligament, similar to the previous steps. The dissection continues on the posterior leaf of the left broad ligament, reaching the upper part of the uterosacral ligament to enhance uterine mobility. The uterine pedicle, including the artery and vein, is skeletonized for better visualization and control.

The surgeon applies bipolar energy to the entire uterine pedicle without cutting it, ensuring control of the opposite side to reduce backflow on the right pedicle. The uterus is tracked to the left, and the peritoneum is opened. After skeletalizing the pedicle, bipolar energy is applied at the isthmic level of the uterus.

The surgeon lateralizes the uterus to the left and cuts the uterine pedicle, starting with the artery followed by the vein. The procedure is bloodless due to prior coagulation. The same steps are repeated on the right side, with the vein being cut medial to the artery.

The surgeon performs a colpotomy using a monopolar needle, beginning the incision on the anterior aspect of the vagina. The section continues along the left lateral aspect, with clear visualization of both anterior and posterior uterine manipulator valves. The incision proceeds to the right lateral aspect and concludes by cutting the posterior aspect of the vagina under precise visual control.

The surgeon extracts the uterus vaginally, opting for hemostasis on the vaginal edges using Vicryl O stitches instead of bipolar hemostasis. The Rodol knot, an autoblockant and quick knot, is used for securing the stitches.

The surgeon begins controlling the vaginal closure by placing interrupted sutures that incorporate both utero-sacral ligaments, aiming to reduce the risk of post-operative vaginal prolapse. The preference is for interrupted stitches over a running suture to minimize ischemia.

The surgeon completes the final knot, ensuring the vagina is entirely closed with minimal use of energy for hemostasis. The right ureter is visually inspected, showing clear ureteral reptation, followed by inspection of the left ureter with visible reptation.

The surgeon performs an ovariopexy by passing a needle through the ovary and the homolateral round ligament to fix the ovary, taking care to avoid the external iliac vessel. This procedure is repeated on the right side.
